From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British actress (1895–1974)

For the Mormon missionary, see [Alma O. Taylor](/wiki/Alma_O._Taylor "Alma O. Taylor").

| Alma Taylor | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Born | Alma Louise Taylor  (1895-01-03)3 January 1895  London, England |
| Died | 23 January 1974(1974-01-23) (aged 79)  London, England |
| Years active | 1907–1958 |
| Spouse | Leonard Avery |

**Alma Louise Taylor** (3 January 1895 – 23 January 1974) was a British actress.

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alma_Taylor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

Taylor was born in [London](/wiki/London "London"). She made her first screen appearance as a [child actor](/wiki/Child_actor "Child actor") in the 1907 film *His Daughter's Voice*. She went on to appear in more than 150 film roles, appearing in a number of larger-budget films such as *[Shadow of Egypt](/wiki/Shadow_of_Egypt "Shadow of Egypt")* which was shot on location in Egypt in 1924. Taylor was one of the major British stars of the 1910s and early 1920s. Most of her films were produced by [Cecil Hepworth](/wiki/Cecil_Hepworth "Cecil Hepworth").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

She is best remembered for her role as Tilly in the long-running *Tilly the Tomboy* series (1910-1915). In 1915 she was voted the most popular British performer by readers of *Pictures and the Picturegoers*, comfortably beating [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") into second place.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

She acted only occasionally after 1932, appearing in films such as *[Lilacs in the Spring](/wiki/Lilacs_in_the_Spring "Lilacs in the Spring")*, *[Blue Murder at St Trinian's](/wiki/Blue_Murder_at_St_Trinian%27s "Blue Murder at St Trinian's")* and *[A Night to Remember](/wiki/A_Night_to_Remember_(1958_film) "A Night to Remember (1958 film)")* during the 1950s. On television, she appeared twice in [*Armchair Theatre*](/wiki/Armchair_Theatre "Armchair Theatre"), in 1957: she played Mrs. Castor and Greta Stenbourg.

She died in London at the age of 79.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
